content:
Summary: Wired the Personal Records screens to the new Supabase-backed backend. Added a dedicated backend PHR module with typed read/create endpoints for BMI, blood pressure, temperature, and medication intake, then updated the Flutter BMI, blood pressure, temperature, and medicine screens to load and save through the API using the authenticated access token. Verified with backend build success and frontend flutter analyze success.

Facts:
1. [finished] The backend now exposes /api/v1/phr endpoints for bmi-records, blood-pressure-records, temperature-records, and medication-intake-records with authenticated read/create flows.
2. [finished] The Flutter BMI, blood pressure, temperature, and medicine intake screens now fetch history from the backend and persist new records instead of using session-only mock lists.
3. [finished] Medicine intake persistence uses structured notes plus the Supabase medicine_intake_records table to preserve display fields like dosage, schedule, next dose, and notes.
4. [finished] The backend build passed after fixing typed insert helper generics and explicit controller return types, and frontend flutter analyze reported no issues.

Affected files:
1. [modified] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\src\app.module.ts — Registered the new PersonalRecordsModule so the backend exposes the PHR routes. | Notes: Imports now include the dedicated PHR module.
2. [modified] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\src\auth\auth.module.ts — Exported AuthSupportService so the new PHR module can reuse authenticated user resolution. | Notes: Keeps auth boundary shared without duplicating token parsing.
3. [created] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\src\personal-records\personal-records.module.ts — Introduced a dedicated module for PHR persistence and controllers. | Notes: Owns the new domain boundary.
4. [created] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\src\personal-records\personal-records.controller.ts — Added typed /phr endpoints for list/create operations across all four record types. | Notes: Controller returns explicit promise types.
5. [created] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\src\personal-records\personal-records.service.ts — Added orchestration between auth resolution and repository mapping for PHR operations. | Notes: Handles per-user access and request normalization.
6. [created] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\src\personal-records\personal-records.repository.ts — Added typed Supabase read/write access for bmi_records, blood_pressure_records, temperature_records, and medication_intake_records. | Notes: Includes typed row mapping and insert helper.
7. [created] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\src\personal-records\personal-records.types.ts — Defined backend response/input contracts for the new PHR endpoints. | Notes: Keeps API shapes explicit.
8. [created] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\src\personal-records\dto\create-bmi-record.dto.ts — Validated BMI create payloads at the API boundary. | Notes: Uses class-validator with numeric coercion.
9. [created] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\src\personal-records\dto\create-blood-pressure-record.dto.ts — Validated blood pressure create payloads at the API boundary. | Notes: Covers systolic/diastolic and optional fields.
10. [created] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\src\personal-records\dto\create-temperature-record.dto.ts — Validated temperature create payloads at the API boundary. | Notes: Supports celsius and fahrenheit inputs.
11. [created] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\src\personal-records\dto\create-medication-intake-record.dto.ts — Validated medication intake create payloads at the API boundary. | Notes: Includes ISO timestamps and status enum validation.
12. [created] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\frontend\lib\features\phr\data\personal_records_api_client.dart — Added a typed HTTP client for the new PHR backend endpoints. | Notes: Includes response parsers for all four record types.
13. [modified] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\frontend\lib\features\phr\body_mass_index\presentation\screen\body_mass_index_screen.dart — Switched BMI history and create flow from local state to backend persistence. | Notes: Keeps the UI composition but loads/saves via the API.

15. [modified] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\frontend\lib\features\phr\blood_pressure\presentation\screen\blood_pressure_screen.dart — Switched blood pressure history and create flow from local state to backend persistence. | Notes: Added backend loading, save flow, and error states.

23. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\src\supabase\database.types.ts — Confirmed the new PHR Supabase table typings already existed and matched the repository code. | Notes: No further changes required in this pass.

content:
Summary: Implemented the first Personal Records persistence slice with separate Supabase tables for BMI, blood pressure, temperature, and medicine intake. Added a new SQL migration, updated typed database contracts, and aligned the schema documentation and backend README with the new tables. Verified the backend still builds successfully after the type updates.

Facts:
1. [finished] A new Supabase migration file was added for Personal Records: BMI, blood pressure, temperature, and medication intake now each have their own table with timestamps, ownership, indexes, and RLS policies.
2. [finished] The backend Supabase database types were expanded to include bmi_records, blood_pressure_records, temperature_records, and medication_intake_records with explicit Insert/Update/Row contracts.
3. [finished] The schema documentation in data-structure.md was updated so the Personal Records section now points to the new separate tables instead of a generic observations-only approach.
4. [finished] backend/README.md now references both the original auth/profile bootstrap script and the new personal-records bootstrap script.
5. [finished] BMI records now include a measurement_system field so the stored record preserves whether the user entered metric or imperial input.

Affected files:
1. [created] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\sqls\006_personal_records_setup.sql — Defines the new persistent database schema for personal records with separate tables, RLS, and indexes. | Notes: Includes BMI, blood pressure, temperature, and medication intake tables.
2. [modified] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\src\supabase\database.types.ts — Adds strongly typed Supabase table contracts for the new personal record tables so backend data access remains type-safe. | Notes: Includes explicit row, insert, and update shapes for each table.
3. [modified] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\data-structure.md — Keeps the architecture document aligned with the implemented personal records schema and the new table-per-screen approach. | Notes: Updated the Personal Records section and BMI notes.
4. [modified] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\README.md — Documents that the new personal records bootstrap script should be run alongside the auth/profile bootstrap script. | Notes: Helps keep setup instructions consistent with the new migration.

content:
Summary: Changed the profile personal information screen from a card-based layout to a flatter BMI-like page structure after the user clarified they wanted no card styling. The screen now uses a plain header, inline profile summary row, divider separation, and flat form sections while keeping the existing save/reset behavior intact.

Facts:
1. [finished] frontend/lib/features/profile/presentation/screens/personal_information_screen.dart now renders without the large summary and form card shells, using flat sections instead.
2. [finished] The personal information screen still preserves the optional showBackButton, wrapWithSafeArea, and centerContent parameters for compatibility while using the new flat layout.
3. [finished] frontend/test/features/profile/presentation/screens/personal_information_screen_test.dart was updated to match the flat section wording and still verifies the main personal information screen content.

Affected files:
1. [modified] frontend/lib/features/profile/presentation/screens/personal_information_screen.dart — Removed the card-like shells and replaced them with a flat header, summary row, divider, and form section. | Notes: Kept the behavior and compatibility parameters unchanged.
2. [modified] frontend/test/features/profile/presentation/screens/personal_information_screen_test.dart — Updated the test description to reflect the flat layout while preserving the main content assertions. | Notes: No functional assertions were removed.

Also updated the widget test to reflect the new card sections and verified the feature with flutter test and flutter analyze.

content:
Summary: Fixed the Security Settings screen crash caused by calling ScaffoldMessenger.of(context) during initState-driven async hydration. The login redirect is now deferred with a post-frame callback, which keeps the cached preloaded Security screen behavior intact and avoids inherited-widget timing errors. Flutter analysis passed after the fix.

Facts:
1. [finished] The Security Settings screen now hydrates from an in-memory user-scoped cache and refreshes silently when cached data exists.
2. [finished] The crash came from _goToLogin() calling ScaffoldMessenger.of(context) before the first frame completed during initState-triggered async work.
3. [finished] _goToLogin() now defers snackbar/navigation work with WidgetsBinding.instance.addPostFrameCallback so it is safe during early screen initialization.
4. [finished] Flutter analyze passed after the deferred-navigation fix with no issues reported.

Affected files:
1. [modified] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\frontend\lib\features\profile\presentation\screens\security_settings_screen.dart — Changed the login redirect to run after the first frame so the screen no longer throws when initState-triggered async work calls ScaffoldMessenger too early. | Notes: The rest of the cache-first loading behavior remains intact.

metadata.summary: Fixed the Security Settings screen crash caused by calling ScaffoldMessenger.of(context) during initState-driven async hydration. The login redirect is now deferred with a post-frame callback, which keeps the cached preloaded Security screen behavior intact and avoids inherited-widget timing errors. Flutter analysis passed after the fix.

content:
Summary: Added a plain-English atlas for WAH4P so future work can read the project like a map. The repo is best understood in layers: a Flutter client that controls navigation and presentation, a NestJS backend that owns security and business rules, and Supabase/Postgres plus secure local storage for persistence. The most important user journeys now have an explicit flow: splash/startup -> onboarding or login -> dashboard or MPIN unlock -> feature areas like records, appointments, resupply, calendar, notifications, profile, and legal screens. This memory also clarifies which screens are static, which are route-driven, and which backend APIs support each major journey.

Facts:
1. [finished] Think of the repository as three layers: frontend/ is the Flutter presentation and navigation layer, backend/ is the NestJS security and business-logic layer, and sqls/ plus the data-structure docs define the Supabase/Postgres persistence layer.
2. [finished] The app begins at the Flutter shell in frontend/lib/app/wah4p_app.dart, which owns theme setup, route generation, startup gating, and resume-time MPIN locking; the visible launch flow is controlled from there rather than from individual screens.
3. [finished] Startup behavior is intentionally simple to follow: the app checks whether a valid session exists, whether onboarding is complete, and whether MPIN unlock is required, then routes the user to onboarding, login, dashboard, or the MPIN unlock screen.
4. [finished] The Flutter route system is centralized in frontend/lib/app/app_routes.dart and frontend/lib/app/app_router.dart, so route names and screen mappings should be edited there first when changing navigation.
5. [finished] The route map forms a clear product atlas: splash and onboarding lead into registration and login, then into the dashboard, and from there into health records, personal records, appointments, medication resupply, calendar, notifications, profile, security settings, and legal/privacy screens.
6. [finished] The frontend feature folders mirror product domains, with separate areas for auth, onboarding, dashboard, health_records, phr, appointments, calendar, medication_resupply, profile, notification, legal, and splash.
7. [finished] The auth feature is one of the largest frontend domains and includes the registration screens, login, email verification, forgot password, MPIN setup/unlock, TOTP challenge/setup, and security verification flows.
8. [finished] The backend app module in backend/src/app.module.ts wires configuration, throttling, the health module, and the auth module, while global guards enforce API-key access and rate limiting across the server.
9. [finished] The backend health endpoint is intentionally public and small, while the rest of the server is protected unless a route is marked public with the custom decorator.
10. [finished] The backend auth module is the server-side workflow hub and splits responsibilities into registration, login, password reset, profile, TOTP, MPIN, and support services/repositories instead of one monolithic auth implementation.
11. [finished] The backend auth controller groups the API by user journey: registration OTP request/verify/complete, password reset request/verify/complete, login and refresh, TOTP setup and verification, security verification, MPIN setup/verification/device registration, and device unregister flow.
12. [finished] The backend profile controller is separate from the login flow and handles authenticated profile retrieval and updates via /profile/me, which matches the frontend profile and personal information screens.
13. [finished] The project’s data rules are to keep canonical patient data in Supabase/Postgres, keep session/onboarding/device flags in secure local storage, and treat dashboard summaries and similar UI aggregates as derived data rather than separate source records.
14. [finished] Some documentation files are secondary references rather than the live truth, especially the older screen inventory; for navigation work, the live Flutter route constants and route builder are the authoritative map.

Affected files:
1. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\frontend\lib\app\wah4p_app.dart — The app shell controls theme, startup gating, and resume-time locking, making it the top-level navigation anchor for the project map. | Notes: Central entry point for the Flutter client.
2. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\frontend\lib\app\app_startup_service.dart — Defines the route decision logic for onboarding, login, dashboard, and MPIN unlock, which is essential to the launch map. | Notes: Startup flow source of truth.
3. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\frontend\lib\app\app_routes.dart — Lists the canonical route names used throughout the Flutter app. | Notes: Best place to understand route coverage.
4. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\frontend\lib\app\app_router.dart — Maps route names to screens and shows transition style choices for each user flow. | Notes: Best source for the screen-to-route atlas.
5. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\frontend\lib\app\startup_gate_screen.dart — Shows the transient gate screen that hands off to the resolved initial route during startup. | Notes: Launch UX and routing handoff.
6. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\frontend\lib\app\app_lock_state_service.dart — Implements the time-based unlock policy used when the app resumes from background. | Notes: Important for security and session behavior.
7. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\frontend\lib\features — The feature directory structure reveals the major product areas and how the client is partitioned by domain. | Notes: Useful for understanding the app map by feature group.
8. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\src\app.module.ts — Shows the backend’s top-level composition, including global guards and core modules. | Notes: Server architecture anchor.
9. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\src\auth\auth.module.ts — Shows the backend auth domain composition across controllers, services, and repositories. | Notes: Primary auth subsystem map.
10. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\src\auth\auth.controller.ts — Exposes the auth workflow endpoints and groups them into clear account/security journeys. | Notes: API surface map for auth.
11. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\src\auth\profile.controller.ts — Provides authenticated profile read/update endpoints separate from the workflow controller. | Notes: Profile API map.
12. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\src\health\health.controller.ts — Shows the public health check route and the small public surface of the server. | Notes: Operational entry point.
13. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\data-structure.md — Defines persistence rules, canonical entities, and the source-of-truth model used by the app. | Notes: Data/storage map.
14. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\checklist.md — Tracks unfinished persistence and validation work across the product and shows which areas still need implementation. | Notes: Project readiness map.
15. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\SCREENS.md — Documents screen inventory and route flow, though it is older and should be reconciled with the live code. | Notes: Secondary reference, not authoritative.

The repo is best understood in layers: a Flutter client that controls navigation and presentation, a NestJS backend that owns security and business rules, and Supabase/Postgres plus secure local storage for persistence. The most important user journeys now have an explicit flow: splash/startup -> onboarding or login -> dashboard or MPIN unlock -> feature areas like records, appointments, resupply, calendar, notifications, profile, and legal screens. This memory also clarifies which screens are static, which are route-driven, and which backend APIs support each major journey.

content:
Summary: Saved a deeper project map for WAH4P that focuses on navigation, feature boundaries, and service responsibilities. The repository presents a clear two-tier architecture: a NestJS backend that owns auth/security/business rules and a Flutter frontend that owns display, navigation, and device-scoped state. The frontend route tree now has a precise map from startup through onboarding, auth, dashboard, PHR, EHR-style records, appointments, calendar, notifications, profile, and legal screens. The backend API surface is also clearer: a public health check plus a broad auth/profile domain that covers registration, login, 2FA, MPIN, password reset, and profile hydration/update. This memory layer is intended to function as an app map, not just a summary.

Facts:
1. [finished] The Flutter client has a single centralized app shell in frontend/lib/app/wah4p_app.dart, and that shell owns the Material theme, navigator key, route generation, startup route wiring, and resume-time MPIN lock behavior.
2. [finished] The app startup path is explicit: frontend/lib/app/app_startup_service.dart resolves the first route based on refreshed auth session, onboarding completion, and MPIN device state, returning onboarding, login, dashboard, or MPIN unlock.
3. [finished] The startup gate screen in frontend/lib/app/startup_gate_screen.dart is only a transient decision screen; it shows loading/status text, then immediately replaces itself with the resolved initial route.
4. [finished] The app lock policy is device-state driven: frontend/lib/app/app_lock_state_service.dart marks the app backgrounded and requires unlock after a five-minute timeout on resume when the user is authenticated.
5. [finished] The route registry is centralized in frontend/lib/app/app_routes.dart, and the route builder in frontend/lib/app/app_router.dart maps every visible screen to a named route with mostly fade, slide, or instant transitions.
6. [finished] The Flutter route map covers these major user journey groups: splash/onboarding, registration, login and MFA, dashboard, health records, personal records, appointments, medication resupply, calendar, notifications, profile, security settings, and legal/privacy.
7. [finished] The frontend feature tree is organized by responsibility under frontend/lib/features/, with dedicated folders for auth, dashboard, health_records, medication_resupply, phr, profile, onboarding, splash, calendar, notification, legal, and appointments.
8. [finished] The backend application module in backend/src/app.module.ts wires configuration, throttling, the auth module, and the health module, while also installing global guards for API-key authorization and rate limiting.
9. [finished] The backend public surface is intentionally small: backend/src/health/health.controller.ts exposes a public GET /health endpoint, while all other routes are protected by the API-key guard unless explicitly marked public.
10. [finished] The backend auth module in backend/src/auth/auth.module.ts is the main domain module and composes controllers plus specialized services for registration, login, password reset, TOTP, MPIN, profile, and supporting repositories.
11. [finished] The backend auth controller groups the API into clear workflow families: registration OTP flow, password reset flow, login and refresh, TOTP setup/challenge, security verification, MPIN set/verify/register/unregister, and device/profile-related auth support.
12. [finished] The backend profile controller in backend/src/auth/profile.controller.ts is separate from the auth workflow endpoints and provides /profile/me GET and PATCH handlers for authenticated profile hydration and updates.
13. [finished] The project docs still matter as design references: data-structure.md defines Supabase/Postgres as the system of record and local secure storage for session/onboarding/device flags, while checklist.md tracks unfinished persistence and validation work.
14. [finished] The current source tree and the older screen inventory docs are not perfectly aligned, so the live Flutter route map should be treated as the authoritative reference for actual navigation until the docs are reconciled.

Affected files:
1. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\frontend\lib\app\wah4p_app.dart — Defines the Flutter app shell, theme, route entrypoint, and resume-time lock behavior, which are central to the app map. | Notes: Confirms where app-wide navigation and lifecycle control live.

metadata.summary: Saved a deeper project map for WAH4P that focuses on navigation, feature boundaries, and service responsibilities. The repository presents a clear two-tier architecture: a NestJS backend that owns auth/security/business rules and a Flutter frontend that owns display, navigation, and device-scoped state. The frontend route tree now has a precise map from startup through onboarding, auth, dashboard, PHR, EHR-style records, appointments, calendar, notifications, profile, and legal screens. The backend API surface is also clearer: a public health check plus a broad auth/profile domain that covers registration, login, 2FA, MPIN, password reset, and profile hydration/update. This memory layer is intended to function as an app map, not just a summary.

content:
Summary: Audited the WAH4P repository and saved a clear project snapshot for future work. The repo is split into a NestJS backend, a Flutter frontend, and supporting SQL/docs. The backend is a secured API with global API-key protection, throttling, strict env validation, Supabase auth/data integration, and Resend email delivery. The frontend is a Flutter mobile app with centralized theming, secure local storage, a startup gate, and MPIN/session lifecycle handling. The docs describe a Supabase/Postgres source of truth for patient data, with frontend screens mostly acting as presentation and interaction layers. I also captured the current testing surface and a couple of documentation mismatches that future work should reconcile.

Facts:
1. [finished] The repository is organized as a multi-part WAH4P project with backend/, frontend/, sqls/, and documentation files at the root, so future changes should treat it as a full-stack product rather than a single app.
2. [finished] The backend is a NestJS API under backend/ that uses Supabase for auth/data, Resend for email OTP delivery, global API-key protection, throttling, and strict Joi-based environment validation.
3. [finished] The backend HTTP entrypoint sets the global API prefix to /api/v1, enables helmet, applies a whitelist/transform ValidationPipe, and reads CORS origins from FRONTEND_ORIGIN.
4. [finished] The frontend is a Flutter app under frontend/ with a centralized Material theme, Open Sans typography, secure storage, and a startup gate flow driven by app state and route generation.
5. [finished] frontend/lib/app/wah4p_app.dart manages app lifecycle behavior, including MPIN lock handling on resume by checking local device state and backend security status before showing the unlock route.
6. [finished] Project docs in data-structure.md and checklist.md define Supabase/Postgres as the canonical source of truth for patient data, while local storage is reserved for session, onboarding, and device-scoped flags.
7. [finished] The repo has active test coverage in both layers: backend has Jest unit/e2e scaffolding, and frontend has multiple widget/screen tests under frontend/test.
8. [finished] SCREENS.md and some docs appear to reflect an older source tree or source-of-truth path, so documentation should be reconciled with the current repository layout before relying on it as the only inventory.

Affected files:
1. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\AGENTS.md — Contains repository-wide operating rules, architecture reminders, and documentation guidance that shape how the project should be understood. | Notes: Established the backend/frontend responsibility split and project conventions.
2. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\data-structure.md — Defines the canonical data model, storage layers, and screen-to-data mapping used by the app. | Notes: Important for understanding persistence and source-of-truth rules.
3. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\checklist.md — Lists the current persistence and validation work items across the app and acts as a project readiness tracker. | Notes: Useful for identifying unfinished foundation work.
4. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\SCREENS.md — Documents the screen inventory and route mapping, which is critical for understanding the frontend surface area. | Notes: Contains a likely path mismatch with the live repo layout.
5. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\package.json — Shows the backend stack, scripts, test tooling, and key runtime dependencies. | Notes: Confirms NestJS, Supabase, Resend, Jest, and validation tooling.
6. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\README.md — Summarizes backend setup, auth endpoints, SQL bootstrap, and local development steps. | Notes: Useful for capturing the intended API surface.
7. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\src\main.ts — Shows backend bootstrap behavior, including security middleware, route prefixing, validation, and CORS configuration. | Notes: Establishes the HTTP entrypoint behavior.
8. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\src\app.module.ts — Defines the backend module composition, including global guards and configuration wiring. | Notes: Confirms API-key and throttling guards are global.
9. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\src\common\config\env.validation.ts — Lists the required environment variables and validation constraints for the backend. | Notes: Critical for deployment and security readiness.
10. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\src\common\guards\api-key.guard.ts — Shows how the backend enforces the x-api-key header and compares secrets safely. | Notes: Important security boundary for all non-public routes.
11. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\frontend\README.md — Documents runtime configuration for the Flutter client and how it connects to the backend. | Notes: Confirms the app uses backend URL and API key configuration.
12. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\frontend\pubspec.yaml — Shows the Flutter SDK version, package dependencies, and bundled assets used by the app. | Notes: Confirms secure storage, HTTP, QR, and local asset usage.
13. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\frontend\analysis_options.yaml — Captures the Flutter lint baseline for the client codebase. | Notes: No custom lint overrides beyond the default Flutter lint set.
14. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\frontend\lib\main.dart — Shows the Flutter application entrypoint and how the app widget is launched. | Notes: Thin entrypoint that delegates to the app shell.
15. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\frontend\lib\app\wah4p_app.dart — Defines the Flutter app shell, theme, route generation, startup gate, and MPIN lock-on-resume behavior. | Notes: Central file for app lifecycle and navigation policy.

The backend is a secured API with global API-key protection, throttling, strict env validation, Supabase auth/data integration, and Resend email delivery. The frontend is a Flutter mobile app with centralized theming, secure local storage, a startup gate, and MPIN/session lifecycle handling. The docs describe a Supabase/Postgres source of truth for patient data, with frontend screens mostly acting as presentation and interaction layers.
